name: System Monitoring Dashboard
description: Build a quick system monitoring overview using standard Linux commands to check CPU, memory, disk, and network status.
trendingReason: Fundamental skill for any server or cloud instance troubleshooting

steps.title: Check system uptime and load average
steps.description: View how long the system has been running and the current load average.
steps.tip: Load average numbers represent 1-min, 5-min, and 15-min averages. Values should be below the number of CPU cores.

steps.title: View memory usage
steps.description: Check total, used, free, and available memory.
steps.tip: Focus on the 'available' column, not 'free'. Linux uses free RAM for disk cache, which is reclaimable.

steps.title: Check disk usage
steps.description: View disk space usage across all mounted filesystems.

steps.title: View top processes by resource usage
steps.description: See the most resource-intensive processes sorted by CPU usage.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# System Monitoring Dashboard
set -euo pipefail

echo "========================================="
echo "  System Monitoring Dashboard"
echo "========================================="

echo ""
echo "--- Hostname & Uptime ---"
hostname
uptime

echo ""
echo "--- CPU Info ---"
nproc && echo "cores"
grep 'model name' /proc/cpuinfo | head -1 | cut -d: -f2

echo ""
echo "--- Memory ---"
free -h

echo ""
echo "--- Disk Usage ---"
df -h | grep -v tmpfs | grep -v udev

echo ""
echo "--- Top 5 Processes (CPU) ---"
ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -6

echo ""
echo "--- Top 5 Processes (Memory) ---"
ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -6

echo ""
echo "--- Network Interfaces ---"
ip -brief addr 2>/dev/null || ifconfig -a 2>/dev/null | grep -E '^[a-z]|inet '

echo ""
echo "========================================="

faq.question: What load average is too high?
faq.answer: A sustained load average above the number of CPU cores indicates the system is overloaded. For example, on a 4-core system, a load above 4.0 means processes are waiting for CPU time.

faq.question: Why does 'free' show very little free memory?
faq.answer: Linux uses available RAM for disk cache (buff/cache). This memory is automatically reclaimed when applications need it. Look at the 'available' column for actual usable memory.
